examples.title: First Come First Serve (FCFS) Scheduling
examples.description: Non-preemptive scheduling algorithm that selects processes in order of their arrival time
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FCFS_Scheduling(processes[])
BEGIN
  n = length(processes)
  // Sort by arrival time
  Sort processes by arrival_time
  
  current_time = 0
  total_wt = 0
  total_tat = 0
  
  FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
    // If process arrives later than current time
    IF processes[i].arrival_time > current_time THEN
      current_time = processes[i].arrival_time
    ENDIF
    
    // Wait time = current time - arrival time
    processes[i].waiting_time = current_time - processes[i].arrival_time
    
    // Execute process
    current_time = current_time + processes[i].burst_time
    
    // Turnaround time = completion time - arrival time
    processes[i].turnaround_time = current_time - processes[i].arrival_time
    
    total_wt = total_wt + processes[i].waiting_time
    total_tat = total_tat + processes[i].turnaround_time
  END FOR
  
  avg_wt = total_wt / n
  avg_tat = total_tat / n
  
  RETURN avg_wt, avg_tat
END
examples.explanation: FCFS schedules processes based on their arrival time in the ready queue. When a process gets the CPU, it runs to completion. The algorithm is simple but can lead to poor performance due to convoy effect.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for sorting, O(n) for scheduling

examples.title: Shortest Job First (SJF) Scheduling
examples.description: Non-preemptive scheduling that selects process with smallest burst time
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SJF_Scheduling(processes[])
BEGIN
  n = length(processes)
  completed = 0
  current_time = 0
  min_burst = INFINITY
  shortest_index = -1
  found = FALSE
  
  WHILE completed < n DO
    // Find shortest burst time among arrived processes
    FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
      IF processes[i].arrival_time <= current_time AND
         processes[i].burst_time < min_burst AND
         processes[i].completed == FALSE THEN
        min_burst = processes[i].burst_time
        shortest_index = i
        found = TRUE
      ENDIF
    END FOR
    
    IF found == FALSE THEN
      current_time = current_time + 1
      CONTINUE
    ENDIF
    
    // Execute the shortest process
    processes[shortest_index].completion_time = current_time + processes[shortest_index].burst_time
    processes[shortest_index].waiting_time = current_time - processes[shortest_index].arrival_time
    processes[shortest_index].turnaround_time = processes[shortest_index].completion_time - processes[shortest_index].arrival_time
    
    processes[shortest_index].completed = TRUE
    completed = completed + 1
    current_time = processes[shortest_index].completion_time
    min_burst = INFINITY
    found = FALSE
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: SJF selects the ready process with the smallest burst time. It provides optimal average waiting time but requires knowledge of burst times in advance. It can cause starvation of long processes.

examples.title: Round Robin Scheduling
examples.description: Preemptive scheduling using time quantum for fair CPU allocation
examples.code:
ALGORITHM RoundRobin(processes[], time_quantum)
BEGIN
  n = length(processes)
  ready_queue = []
  current_time = 0
  
  // Add all processes arriving at time 0
  FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
    IF processes[i].arrival_time == 0 THEN
      ready_queue.enqueue(processes[i])
      processes[i].remaining_time = processes[i].burst_time
    ENDIF
  END FOR
  
  WHILE ready_queue is not empty DO
    current_process = ready_queue.dequeue()
    
    // Execute for time quantum or remaining time
    IF current_process.remaining_time <= time_quantum THEN
      current_time = current_time + current_process.remaining_time
      current_process.completion_time = current_time
      current_process.remaining_time = 0
    ELSE
      current_time = current_time + time_quantum
      current_process.remaining_time = current_process.remaining_time - time_quantum
    ENDIF
    
    // Add newly arrived processes to queue
    FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
      IF processes[i].arrival_time > (current_time - time_quantum) AND
         processes[i].arrival_time <= current_time AND
         NOT processes[i].in_queue THEN
        ready_queue.enqueue(processes[i])
      ENDIF
    END FOR
    
    // If process not completed, add back to queue
    IF current_process.remaining_time > 0 THEN
      ready_queue.enqueue(current_process)
    ELSE
      current_process.turnaround_time = current_process.completion_time - current_process.arrival_time
      current_process.waiting_time = current_process.turnaround_time - current_process.burst_time
    ENDIF
  END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Round Robin gives each process equal time slices in a cyclic manner. It ensures fairness and good response time but has overhead from context switches if quantum is too small.

examples.title: Process Control Block Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of PCB data structure in Python
examples.code:
class PCB:
    def __init__(self, pid, name, priority=0):
        self.pid = pid              # Process ID
        self.name = name            # Process Name
        self.state = "NEW"          # Current state
        self.priority = priority    # Scheduling priority
        
        # CPU Registers context
        self.program_counter = 0   # Next instruction to execute
        self.registers = []        # CPU register values
        
        # Memory management
        self.memory_limits = {}    # Memory boundaries
        self.page_table = {}       # Virtual memory pages
        
        # Scheduling info
        self.arrival_time = 0
        self.burst_time = 0
        self.remaining_time = 0
        
        # Execution metrics
        self.waiting_time = 0
        self.turnaround_time = 0
        self.completion_time = 0
        
        # I/O Status
        self.open_files = []
        self.io_devices = []
        self.io_operations = []
        
        # Parent-Child relationship
        self.parent_pid = None
        self.children = []
    
    def context_save(self, cpu_state):
        """Save current CPU state to PCB"""
        self.registers = cpu_state.registers
        self.program_counter = cpu_state.pc
    
    def context_restore(self, cpu_state):
        """Restore CPU state from PCB"""
        cpu_state.registers = self.registers
        cpu_state.pc = self.program_counter
    
    def __str__(self):
        return f"PCB(PID:{self.pid}, State:{self.state}, Priority:{self.priority})"

# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    pcb = PCB(1001, "Chrome", priority=5)
    pcb.state = "READY"
    pcb.burst_time = 50
    pcb.remaining_time = 50
    pcb.arrival_time = 0
    print(pcb)
examples.explanation: This Python class demonstrates how a Process Control Block stores all necessary information about a process. It includes process state, CPU context, memory info, scheduling parameters, and I/O status.
